channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Pulled in at Least $2.2 Billion in 2025, Financial Disclosure Shows
item.description: The release of a mandatory financial disclosure for 2025 shows that the Trump family’s holdings, particularly the president’s crypto businesses, were stunningly lucrative.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess, Andrea Fuller, Eric Lipton and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump entering the White House on Sunday. His mandatory financial disclosure report for 2025 pulled back the curtain on his secretive business operations.

item.title: Trump’s Moneymaking Run: Unrivaled in Presidential History
item.description: The president’s move to open new business ventures, rather than eliminate potential conflicts, defies a long-held tradition.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: The bulk of the new revenue for President Trump comes from the cryptocurrency industry, which his family aggressively moved into starting in late 2024.

item.title: The Supreme Court’s Momentous Term, and How Trump Has Made at Least $2.2 Billion in Office
item.description: Plus, zombies at the mall.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court on Tuesday said states were free to bar transgender girls from competing in girls’ and women’s school sports.

item.title: Control of the Senate Is Up for Grabs, Times/Siena Polls Find
item.description: Republicans are defending seats in Alaska, Iowa, Maine, North Carolina, Ohio and Texas as they try to maintain their majority. Democrats are competitive in all six states — but not leading in enough to take the chamber.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.media:credit: Edison Wu/The New York Times

item.title: Takeaways From the Colorado Primary Elections: Another Win for Democratic Socialists
item.description: A democratic socialist ousted a veteran congresswoman in Denver, and a U.S. senator lost his bid for governor. But the state’s other senator fended off a progressive primary challenger.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melat Kiros, center, toppled Representative Diana DeGette, a veteran Democrat in Denver.

item.title: Left-Wing Insurgent Ousts 15-Term Congresswoman in Colorado
item.description: Melat Kiros, a 29-year-old democratic socialist, unseated Representative Diana DeGette in a Democratic primary to represent the Denver area.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melat Kiros, an immigrant and first-time candidate, is likely to head to Congress in a deep-blue Colorado district.

item.title: Ohio and Iowa Governors Races Tight, New Polls Show
item.description: Voters polled in the states, Iowa and Ohio, also voiced high levels of disapproval for President Trump’s performance.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman and Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: KC McGinnis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters cast their ballots in a primary election in Des Moines in early June.

item.title: Despite Some Losses for Trump, Supreme Court Delivers Enduring Conservative Wins
item.description: The justices pushed back on some of President Trump’s signature moves, but they also expanded presidential power and supplied victories on long-sought conservative goals.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court struck down President Trump’s order to end the guarantee of birthright citizenship for the U.S.-born babies of undocumented immigrants and temporary visitors.

item.title: The Birthright Decision Was Surprisingly Close, Some Legal Scholars Say
item.description: A bare majority of Supreme Court justices ruled that President Trump’s executive order was unconstitutional, reflecting a conservative shift on the issue.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Birthright citizenship as a constitutional right survived by one vote.

item.title: Takeaways From a Transformative Supreme Court Term
item.description: The justices rejected some of President Trump’s signature initiatives, but delivered lasting, long-sought conservative wins.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court on Tuesday said states were free to bar transgender girls from competing in girls’ and women’s school sports.

item.title: Heat Wave Trudges East on Wednesday
item.description: More than 160 million people are under extreme heat warnings or heat advisories ahead of the Fourth of July weekend.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar, Erin McCann and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times

item.title: With Lessons From a Perilous Blizzard, Mamdani Prepares for Extreme Heat
item.description: After more than two dozen people died during a brutal cold snap in New York City, city officials came up with a plan to keep people safe in the cold and extreme heat.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman and Caitlyn Freeman
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani held a news conference on extreme heat preparedness at New York City Emergency Management Headquarters.

item.title: 5 Temperature Records Meteorologists Are Watching This Week
item.description: New York could record 100 degrees for the first time since 2012, as a brutal heat wave spreads east.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Washington might break records for heat this week.

item.title: How War Takes Its Toll on Ukraine’s Pregnant Women
item.description: Bombings, blackouts and displacement compound maternal anxiety. Still, many women persevere. “We must bring new life,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Addario, Maria Varenikova and Oleksandra Mykolyshyn
item.media:credit: Lynsey Addario for The New York Times

item.title: Back in Congress, Tom Kean Jr. Faces a New Challenge: Staying There
item.description: The New Jersey Republican has explained the reason for his long absence: depression. Now he must convince voters that he deserves another term.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr. of New Jersey.

item.title: What Tom Kean Did and Didn’t Explain About His Absence
item.description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr., a New Jersey Republican, has finally reappeared in Congress. Questions remain about his nearly four-month absence from public life, which he said was due to depression.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr., Republican of New Jersey, left many questions unanswered in his speech to the House of Representatives on Tuesday.

item.title: For Many Americans, Depression Is Familiar. Here’s What We Know.
item.description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr. announced he had been hospitalized for depression. More than one in four U.S. adults report having been diagnosed with the condition.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Goldstein and Dana G. Smith
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr. appeared on Capitol Hill on Tuesday following a lengthy absence.

item.title: Trump Suggestion of a Syrian Crackdown on Hezbollah Confounds Many in Mideast
item.description: During peace talks, President Trump repeatedly floated the idea that Syria could help subdue Hezbollah in Lebanon. The proposal revived bitter memories.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum and Raja Abdulrahim
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: A man in the village of Nabatieh al Fawka, Lebanon. He returned earlier this week after fleeing heavy fighting between Israeli forces and Hezbollah in the area.

item.title: U.S. Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Most Powerful A.I. Models
item.description: The move allows Anthropic to bring its most powerful technologies back online, de-escalating a feud with the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic’s Code With Claude event in San Francisco last month. Federal restrictions were lifted on Tuesday from the company’s Claude Mythos and Claude Fable A.I. models.

item.title: Fact-Checking Trump’s Inaccurate Accounts of U.S. History
item.description: We fact-checked the president’s colorful — and inaccurate — accounts of American history from the Battle of Gettysburg to the Unabomber.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu and Jamie Leventhal
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Since retaking office, President Trump has spun many historical yarns that stretch the truth, strain credulity or are downright false.

item.title: Victor Willis, Lead Singer of the Village People, Dies at 74
item.description: Often performing dressed as a helmeted police officer, he also co-wrote the group’s biggest hits, including “Y.M.C.A.” and “Macho Man.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Paul Natkin/Getty Images
item.media:description: Victor Willis performing with the Village People in Chicago in 1979. More recently, he became a polarizing figure because of his (and the group’s music’s) association with President Trump.

item.title: U.S. Mobilizes for Venezuela Despite Trump’s Disdain for Foreign Aid
item.description: The State Department has promised $100 million in new funds to aid groups, after President Trump was criticized for an anemic response to an earthquake in Myanmar last year.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Edward Wong
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers and family members searched for survivors amid several collapsed residential buildings in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Monday. Two powerful earthquakes struck the region last week.

item.title: After Venezuela Earthquakes, Deportees from the U.S. Are Missing or Found Dead
item.description: Many of the 146 Venezuelans deported from the United States the day the earthquake struck are feared dead.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Patricia Sulbarán
item.media:credit: Vantor, via Reuters
item.media:description: The damaged state-run holding facility for deportees, on a hilltop in La Guaira, Venezuela, was meant to be a way station for a group that arrived there on Wednesday, June 26. Most of them never made it home.

item.title: After a Tap Heard in Rubble, U.S. Rescue Teams Begin a Grueling Marathon
item.description: Surrounded by the devastation of Venezuela’s earthquakes, emergency specialists from California, Virginia and Florida work with locals to search for survivors.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler and Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Matias Delacroix/Associated Press
item.media:description: Members of the Fairfax County Urban Search and Rescue team from Virginia searched for people in the rubble of a collapsed building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Sunday.

item.title: A Risky Burial in the Heart of an Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Safe interment is critical to containing the deadly disease, but in recent days around one Congolese town, that was not always being done.
item.dc:creator: Finbarr O’Reilly
item.media:credit: Finbarr O’Reilly for The New York Times

item.title: The Dress Code for Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s Wedding? Expect a Black-Tie Event.
item.description: New details have emerged about the celebration at Madison Square Garden, widely believed to be a wedding, including a dress code.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.media:credit: Gareth Cattermole/TAS24, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Travis Kelce’s white-tie ensemble here would be a touch too formal for a black-tie event.

item.title: Five Words That Shook the World
item.description: It wasn’t Thomas Jefferson who made “All men are created equal” actually mean something.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie
item.media:credit: Naila Ruechel for The New York Times

item.title: How to World Cup
item.description: New to soccer? You’re welcome here.
item.dc:creator: Roger Bennett
item.category: United States Men's National Soccer Team

item.title: A Separation of Powers
item.description: We look at the Supreme Court rulings on birthright citizenship and transgender athletes.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times

item.title: What Older Adults Should Know About GLP-1 Drugs as Medicare Coverage Expands
item.description: A Medicare pilot beginning Wednesday will broaden access to medications like Wegovy and Zepbound. There are unique risks for those over 65.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum
